relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Melchizedek appears as king of peace and priest of the Most High.
His name speaks justice, and Salem speaks peace.
Without genealogy in the story, he images an abiding priesthood.
Abraham’s tithe reveals Melchizedek’s greatness.
Levitical priests receive tithes from siblings under Law.
Melchizedek receives from Abraham and blesses the promise-bearer.
The lesser is blessed by the greater.
Here dying priests receive; there one is witnessed as living.
Levi is seen as tithing through Abraham.
Levi was still within Abraham when Melchizedek met him.
The question arises: why another priestly order?
A changed priesthood means a changed law-order.
The spoken one comes from another tribe.
The Lord arises from Judah.
Melchizedek’s likeness clarifies the new priesthood.
This priesthood rests on indestructible life.
The Psalm says: priest into the age.
The prior command is set aside in this priestly argument.
A better hope draws us near to Divine.
This priesthood arrives with oath.
The Lord swore: priest into the age.
Jesus becomes guarantor of better covenant.
Many priests were stopped by death.
He remains and holds permanent priesthood.
He saves completely, always living to intercede.
Such a high priest fits our need: holy, harmless, undefiled.
He offers himself once for all.
The oath appoints the completed Son into the age.

contemplative_note: Hebrews 7 is priesthood of indestructible life: justice and peace meet in Melchizedek’s pattern, and Jesus lives always to intercede.
